name: glaucoma
description: group of eye diseases that damage the optic nerve, often due to high eye pressure
causes: fluid (aqueous humor) fails to drain properly

name: mononucleosis
description: a contagious infection typically caused by the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)
cure: no specific cure; focused on relieving symptoms with rest, fluids, and over-the-counter pain relievers

name: arteriosclerosis
description: the hardening and stiffening of the arteries

types.name: atherosclerosis
types.description: the most common type, where fatty deposits called plaque build up inside larger arteries, eventually blocking blood flow
causes: This process begins when fats, like lipid (LDL cholesterol), build up in the artery walls, attracting inflammatory cells that damage the artery. Over time, this damage allows more plaque to accumulate.
types.prognosis: can lead to complications like heart attack, stroke, aneurysm, congestive heart failure, and cardiac arrest

types.name: arteriolosclerosis
types.description: affects the small arteries, or arterioles, which thicken, often due to conditions like hypertension and diabetes; these smaller blood vessels help manage your blood pressure and carry blood to organs like the kidneys
types.prognosis: when arterioles fail, organs may not receive adequate blood flow, leading to potential health complications

types.name: medial arterial calcification
types.correlations: often associated with kidney failure and hemodialysis
types.description: calcium builds up in the middle layer of artery walls, making them hard
types.prognosis: it can lead to problems with blood flow and heart health

where-types.name: coronary artery disease
where-types.description: hardening occurs in the coronary arteries, which supply blood to the heart

where-types.name: peripheral artery disease
where-types.description: hardening affects the arteries in the limbs or pelvis, usually leading to issues with blood flow in the legs

where-types.name: carotid artery disease
where-types.description: hardening occurs in the carotid arteries, which carry blood to the brain and can increase stroke risk

where-types.name: aortic aneurysm
where-types.description: hardening occurs in the aorta, the large artery that runs from the heart down to the pelvis, and can lead to weakening or bulging of the vessel

causes: the buildup of fatty, lipid-rich deposits, (plaque) along the artery walls
prognosis: over time, these plaques can become fibrotic and calcified, further contributing to arterial stiffness
this hardening of the arteries can restrict blood flow and cause other problems, such as heart disease
complications of arteriosclerosis are often serious, even deadly: heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, amputation, and pulmonary embolism
prevention:
Most arteriosclerosis risk factors can be controlled through lifestyle choices.
An active lifestyle and healthy diet can keep you in good shape and lower your likelihood of heart attack, stroke, obesity, type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, and high blood lipids. Giving up tobacco and limiting alcohol consumption can also lower your risk of arteriosclerosis.
Unfortunately, some risk factors—age and family history, for instance—are out of your control. However, if you have risk factors like a family history of the condition, it can be a red flag for arteriosclerosis and a sign that you should get checked, even if you don’t have symptoms.

diagnosis.name: exercise stress tests
diagnosis.discussion: exercise on a treadmill or on a stationary bicycle can identify the presence of low blood flow in the heart in many patients, indicating a potential blockage

diagnosis.name: nuclear scans
diagnosis.discussion: nuclear scans—like radionuclide angiography and myocardial perfusion—can measure the rate at which your heart moves blood and identify parts of your heart that don't get enough blood, which can be signs that your arteries aren't working as they should

diagnosis.name: imaging
diagnosis.discussion: X-rays and CT scans can show blockages or coronary or artery calcification

diagnosis.name: blood pressure differential
diagnosis.discussion: if blood pressure in your arms and legs is noticeably different, it may be a sign of blocked arteries

diagnosis.name: cardiac catheterization
diagnosis.discussion: in some cases, testing may lead to cardiac catheterization, a procedure in which the doctor uses a thin tube inserted into your blood vessels to inject a dye that helps the X-ray better detect and potentially treat blockages in the same procedure

cure.name: lifestyle changes
cure.discussion: giving up tobacco, eating a heart-healthy diet, and getting more exercise can help by lowering your blood pressure, improving your blood glucose levels, and lowering cholesterol

cure.name: medication
cure.discussion: you may treat your arteriosclerosis with medications that help improve your blood flow (such as blood pressure drugs and blood thinners) or reduce the likelihood of plaque build-up (like anti-platelet and cholesterol-lowering meds)

cure.name: surgery
cure.discussion: in severe cases, treatment may include percutaneous interventions, such as angioplasty or stenting, or, in some instances, surgery. For more complex cases, bypass surgery may be recommended. This involves using a healthy blood vessel from another part of the body to create a new path for blood to flow around the blockage. Depending on the location of the blockage, this may be done as coronary artery bypass surgery or peripheral artery bypass surgery
